title: A Theory of Saddle Escape in Deep Nonlinear Networks
abstract: In deep networks with small initialization, training exhibits long plateaus separated by sharp feature-acquisition transitions. Whereas shallow nonlinear networks and deep linear networks are well studied, extending these analyses to deep nonlinear networks remains challenging. We derive an exact identity for the imbalance of Frobenius norms of layer weight matrices that holds for any smooth activation and any differentiable loss and use this to classify activation functions into four universality classes. On the permutation-symmetric submanifold, the identity combines with an approximate balance law to reduce the full matrix flow to a scalar ODE, giving a critical-depth escape time law $τ_\star = Θ(\varepsilon^{-(r-2)})$ governed by the number $r$ of layers at the bottleneck scale rather than the total depth $L$. We find that this same $r-2$ exponent is recovered under He-normal initialization with $r$ bottleneck layers rescaled by $\varepsilon$, where the symmetry manifold is preserved by the flow but not attracting. We find close agreement between our theory and numerical simulations.
summary: This paper provides a theoretical framework for understanding why deep neural networks experience long training plateaus followed by sudden feature acquisition. The authors prove that the time spent in these plateaus is governed by the number of layers at the small-scale 'bottleneck' rather than the total depth of the network. By classifying activation functions into universality classes, they show that the escape dynamics follow a predictable power-law scaling. The theory is robust, applying to both symmetric and asymmetric initializations and extending to multi-mode teacher scenarios.
main_problem: Understanding the dynamical mechanism behind 'saddle escape' in deep nonlinear networks, specifically how the time required to escape training plateaus scales with network depth and initialization scale.
main_result: The discovery of a critical-depth escape law where escape time scales as epsilon^-(r-2), where r is the number of bottleneck layers, rather than the total network depth L.
method: Derivation of an exact identity for weight matrix norm imbalance, reduction of high-dimensional matrix gradient flow to a scalar ODE via a symmetric balanced ansatz, and use of Hermite-moment expansions.
model_or_system: Deep nonlinear feedforward networks with L layers, smooth activation functions (classified into four universality classes), and Gaussian inputs, trained via gradient flow on a population squared loss.
key_observables: Escape time (tau* or t_esc), layer weight Frobenius norms, signal energy (gamma), and layer scale imbalance (Delta_l).
important_parameters: Initialization scale (epsilon), bottleneck depth (r), total network depth (L), and nonlinearity order (q).
main_assumptions: Small-scale initialization (epsilon -> 0), gradient flow dynamics, Gaussian input distribution, and the use of a permutation-symmetric submanifold for reduction.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares reduced ODE loss to full-parameter GD; Figure 3 shows universality collapse of different activations; Figure 4 shows log-log plots of escape time vs epsilon confirming the power-law scaling; Figure 5 illustrates multi-mode escape dynamics.
paper_structure: The paper begins by deriving an exact identity for weight imbalance, introduces a reduction method to a scalar ODE, classifies activation functions, proves the critical-depth escape law for single-mode and multi-mode teachers, and validates the theory via numerical simulations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Empirical confirmation of ansatz reduction. (a) Loss vs. time: reduced ODE (solid line) overlaid with empirical NL-parameter gradient descent (circles). (b) Layer scales Xℓversus time t: scalar ODE trajectories match the full-parameter dynamics.

Figure 2: Escape time obeys critical-depth law on the manifold. (a) Escape time tesc vs initializa- tion scale ε for balanced init: closed form (solid) and reduced ODE (diamonds), polynomial scaling ε−(L−2) steepens with depth. (b) Same at fixed L = 6 with r layers at bottleneck scale: diamonds track the ε−(r−2) law of Theorem 6. Theoretical prediction and experiment diverge at large ε.

Figure 3: Universality across activations. (a) Raw escape time tesc vs ε for three Class B activations (tanh, erf, sin; solid line) and two Class C (GELU, Swish; dashed). (b) After rescaling by K(σ): Class B curves collapse onto the master curve of Corollary 7; Class C deviates by O(γC ε) per Section C.
Figure 4: Off-manifold critical-depth exponent. tesc vs ε for L = 8 tanh with r ∈{3, 5, 8} bot- tleneck layers, He-normal init, SGD: slopes track the ε−(r−2) law of Theorem 11.
Figure 5: Three-mode tanh cascade and escape-time decomposition. Black: training loss; blue: mode-1 alignment ∥W1v1∥2/ √
